title: Colligative Properties and Abnormal Molecular Masses

concepts.title: Colligative Properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Colligative Properties

Certain properties of solutions depend only on the number of particles of the solute (molecular or ions) present in definite volume of the solution and do not depend on the nature of solute. Such properties are called colligative properties.

A few colligative properties are:

(i) Relative lowering of vapour pressure

(ii) Elevation of boiling point

(iii) Depression in freezing point

(iv) Osmotic pressure

concepts.title: Relative lowering of vapour pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
(i) Relative lowering of vapour pressure:

The relative lowering of vapour pressure is a colligative property as it depends only on the concentration of the solute and it is independent of its nature.

Consider a binary solution consisting of non volatile solute in a volatile solvent. The vapour pressure of the solution $(\text{P})$ is equal to vapor pressure of solvent $\left( {{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}} \right)$ in the solution, which in turn is directly proportional to its mole fraction in solution.

$\text{P }={{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}$

$=\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}{{\chi }_{\text{A}}}$ … (i)

where ${{\chi }_{\text{A}}}=$ mole fraction of solvent

If mole fraction of solute is ${{\chi }_{B}}$ then

${{\chi }_{\text{A}}}+{{\chi }_{\text{B}}}=1\text{ or }{{\chi }_{\text{A}}}=1-{{\chi }_{\text{B}}}$ … (ii)

From eq. (i) and (ii)

${{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}=\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}\left( 1-{{\chi }_{\text{B}}} \right)=\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}{{\chi }_{\text{B}}}$

$\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}=\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}{{\chi }_{\text{B}}}$

$\frac{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}={{\chi }_{\text{B}}}$

$\frac{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}={{\chi }_{\text{B}}}$

is called the relative lowering of vapor pressure and is equal to the mole fraction of the solute.

Now, $\quad \frac{\Delta \text{P}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}={{\chi }_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{n}}_{\text{A}}}+{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}}=\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{n}}_{\text{A}}}}\quad $ {for dilute solutions: ${{\text{n}}_{\text{A}}}+{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}\approx {{\text{n}}_{\text{A}}}$}

concepts.title: Calculation of molecular mass from relative lowering of vapor pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
Calculation of molecular mass from relative lowering of vapor pressure

A known mass ${{W}_{B}}$ of the non-volatile solute is dissolved in a known mass $\left( {{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}} \right)$ of the volatile solvent to prepare a dilute solution and relative lowering of vapor pressure is determined experimentally. Knowing the molecular mass $\left( {{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}} \right)$ of the solvent, molecular mass $\left( {{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}} \right)$ of the solute can be determined as follows:

${{\text{n}}_{\text{A}}}=\frac{{{\text{w}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}},{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}$

${{\text{X}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{n}}_{\text{A}}}+{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}+{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}$

Relative lowering of vapor pressure is given by

$\frac{\Delta \text{P}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}=\frac{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}={{\text{X}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}+{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}$

For dilute solutions ${{W}_{B}}/{{M}_{B}}\ll {{W}_{A}}/{{M}_{A}}$ and hence in the above expression ${{W}_{B}}/{{M}_{B}}$ may be neglected in the denominator as compared with ${{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}$.

$\frac{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}/{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}}$

${{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{W}}_{\text{B}}}{{\text{M}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{W}}_{\text{A}}}}\times \left( \frac{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}}{\text{P}_{\text{A}}^{0}-{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}}} \right)$

In this expression all the parameters are known except ${{M}_{B}}$ and hence ${{M}_{B}}$ can be calculated.

concepts.title: Elevation of boiling point
concepts.conceptBody:
Elevation Of Boiling Point

The boiling point of a liquid may be defined as the temperature at which the vapor pressure of the liquid becomes equal to the atmospheric pressure.

The vapor pressure curves for the pure solvent and solution consisting of a non-volatile solute is represented diagrammatically.$\text{AB}$ is the vapor pressure curve for pure solvent and $\text{CD}$ is vapor pressure curve for the solution. ${{\text{T}}_{0}}$ represents the boiling point of pure solvent and ${{\text{T}}_{1}}$ represents the boiling point of solution. In the presence of a non-volatile solute the solution has to be heated to a slightly higher temperature so that the vapor of the solution becomes equal to atmospheric pressure and solution begins to boil. The differences in the boiling points are represented as $\Delta {{T}_{b}}$, which is the elevation in boiling point.

$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\text{m}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$: molal boiling point elevation constant or ebullioscopic constant of solvent

$\text{m}$: molality of the solution

Hence, $\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\left( \frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{g}}_{\text{A}}}}\times 1000 \right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad \Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{1000~{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{{\text{g}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{g}}_{\text{A}}}{{\text{M}}_{\text{B}}}}$

concepts.title: Boiling Point Elevation Constant (Kb)
concepts.conceptBody:
Boiling Point Elevation Constant $\left( {{\mathbf{K}}_{\text{b}}} \right)$:

 It is the elevation in point achieved when a solution of a non-volatile solute of concentration 1 molal is boiled. Its boiling units are $\text{Kkgmo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$. ${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$ is related to molar enthalpy of vaporization as

${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{\text{MRT}_{\text{b}}^{2}}{\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{vap}}}\times 1000}$

where $\text{M }=\text{ molar mass of solvent,}$

$\text{R }=\text{ Universal gas constant,}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}\text{ }=\text{ boiling point of pure solvent and }$

$\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{vap}}}\text{ }=\text{ enthalpy of vaporization of solvent}\text{. }$

concepts.title: Depression in freezing point
concepts.conceptBody:
Depression In Freezing Point

Freezing point of a substance is defined as the temperature at which the vapor pressure of the solid form is in equilibrium with liquid form of the substance. In other words, solid and liquid forms of the substance has same vapor pressure. Since the vapour pressure of a solution is less than that of pure solvent, the freezing point of the solution will be lower than that of pure solvent.

Let $\Delta {{T}_{f}}$ be the depression in freezing point, then

$\Delta {{T}_{\text{f}}}={{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\text{T}}_{0}}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}$: freezing point of solution

${{\text{T}}_{0}}$: freezing point of pure solvent

$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}\text{m}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}}$: molal boiling point elevation constant or cryoscopic constant of solvent.

m: molality of solution

concepts.title: Final point Depression Constant (Kf)
concepts.conceptBody:
Final point Depression Constant (${{K}_{f}}$):

 It is the depression in freezing point achieved when a solution of a non-volatile solute of concentration 1 molal is frozen. Its units are K kg $mo{{l}^{-1}}$. ${{K}_{f}}$ is related to the molar enthalpy of fusion as

${{K}_{f}}=\frac{MRT_{1}^{2}}{\Delta {{H}_{fusion}}\times 1000}$

where $\text{M }=\text{ molar mass of solvent, }$

$\text{R }=\text{ universal gas constant}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{f}}}\text{ }=\text{ freezing point of pure solvent and}$

$\Delta {{\text{H}}_{\text{fusion }}}\text{ }=\text{ enthalpy of fusion of solid solvent }$

concepts.title: Osmotic pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
Osmotic Pressure

Osmosis is defined as the spontaneous flow of solvent into solution or from a more dilute to a more concentrated solution, when the two solutions are separated by a suitable membrane. The membrane allows the free passage of the solvent but not of the dissolved substances. Membranes possessing this property are said to be semipermeable.

There has been some tendency to think pressure as a pressure produced by the solution, but this point of view is misleading. The osmotic pressure is brought into existence only when the solution is separated from the solvent by a semipermeable membrane. The resulting osmosis or tendency for osmosis to occur then produces an excess pressure in the solution. When this excess pressure attains the value of the osmosis pressure, the tendency for solvent to enter the solution is exactly counterbalanced by the reverse tendency and a condition of equilibrium results.

For the accurate measurement of osmotic pressure, it is necessary to have a membrane that is perfectly semipermeable. Artificial membranes made of copper ferrocyanide, $\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}}$, is the best material for osmosis.

The osmotic pressure of a dilute solution of a non-volatile solute is given by $\pi \text{V}=\text{nRT}$

$\pi =\frac{\text{n}}{\text{V}}\text{RT}$

$\pi =\frac{\text{W}}{\text{M}}\frac{\text{RT}}{\text{V}}$

where $\pi $ is the osmotic pressure in atmospheres, $n=$ number of moles of solute $\left( \frac{w}{M} \right)$ in $V$ litres of solution at temperature $\text{T}$ (Kelvin), $\text{R}=0.0821\text{Li}{{\text{t}}^{\text{atm}}}{{\text{K}}^{-1}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$. $\text{C}$ is the molarity of the solution. Measurement of osmotic pressure provides a method of determining molecular weights of proteins, polymer and other macromolecules.

If two solutions are of equal concentrations at the same temperature, they have the same osmotic pressure and they are said to be isotonic. If two solutions have unequal osmotic pressures, the more concentrated solution is said to be hypertonic and the more dilute solution is described as hypo tonic. To study the contents of red blood cells, which are protected by a semipermeable membrane, a technique called haemolysis is used. The red blood cells are placed in a hypo tonic solution, when water moves into the cells. The cells swell and eventually burst releasing the haemoglobin and other molecules.

When a bacterial cell is placed in a hypertonic solution (high concentration), the intracellular water tends to move out of the cell by osmosis. This causes the cell to shrink.

concepts.title: Dissociation
concepts.conceptBody:
Van Hoff Factor ( i )

Since colligative properties depend on the number of particles of the solute, in some cases where the solute associates or dissociates in solution, abnormal results for colligative properties are obtained.

Dissociation: 

Let us consider the case of acetic acid dissolved in water. Let the number of mole of acetic acid dissolved be ‘n ‘.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

Initial no. of mole n

After dissociation $\text{n}(1-\alpha )$ $\text{n}\alpha $ $\text{n}\alpha $

Total mole of solute $\text{= n}(1+\alpha )$

Theoretical colligative property (for example $\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}$ ) based on the number of mole of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ added)

$\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\text{m}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\quad \frac{\text{n}}{\text{W}}\times 1000\text{, where W is the mass of solvent in gm}\text{. }$

Experimental colligative property would be given by

$\Delta \text{T}_{\text{b}}^{\prime }={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{{\text{m}}^{\prime }}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\quad \frac{\text{n}(1+\alpha )}{\text{W}}\times 1000$

$\frac{\text{ Experiment colligative property }}{\text{ Theoretical colligative property }}=\frac{n(1+\alpha )}{n}=(1+\alpha )=i,$ Van’t Hoff factor

concepts.title: Association
concepts.conceptBody:
Association:

 Let us consider the case of acetic acid dissolved in benzene. Let the number of mole of acetic acid dissolved be ‘ $n$ ‘ it forms dimer in benzene.

$2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}\rightleftharpoons {{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)}_{2}}$

Initial no. of mole n

After dissociation $\text{n}(1-\alpha )$ $\text{n}\alpha /2$

Total mole of solute $\text{= n}(1-\alpha /2)$

Theoretical colligative property (for example $\Delta {{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}$ ) (based on the no. of mole of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ added) $\Delta {{T}_{b}}={{K}_{b}}m={{K}_{b}}\quad \frac{n}{W}\times 1000$, where $W$ is the mass of solvent in gm.

Experimental colligative property would be given by

$\Delta \text{T}_{\text{b}}^{\prime }={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}{{\text{m}}^{\prime }}={{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\quad \frac{\text{n}\left( 1-\frac{\alpha }{2} \right)}{\text{W}}\times 1000$

$\frac{\text{ Experimental colligative property }}{\text{ Theoretical colligative property }}=\frac{n\left( 1-\frac{\alpha }{2} \right)}{n}=1-\frac{\alpha }{2}=i,\text{Van't Hoff factor}$

Therefore, for dissociation $i>1$ and for association $i<1$. When a solute neither dissociates nor associates, the Van’t Hoff factor is equal to 1.
